item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see that there's a specific story being told here, but it's heavily overshadowed by the fact that this is SCPified Lovecraft. This articles takes his deities, which weren't characters but plot and thematical tools, and turns them into big scary monsters. The nuance and themes of the original creatures are replaced with superpower-esque abilities and generic descriptions that have been spread by the internet in the last several years. Each creature was hand-crafted by Lovecraft to depict the specific themes of its stories, jumbling them into one narrative misses that point and makes them interchangeable with any random monster.

I don't see why this couldn't have been its own mythology. The Dreamlands are hardly a concept unique Lovecraft, so there's no reason why you couldn't have written your own setting taking place inside of dreams.

There's other problems I have with the piece (tonal issues, Lovecraft not sounding like Lovecraft) but the aforementioned issue already ruins the skip for me.

-1, even though this has more effort and ability than a coldpost, it feels the same as someone writing Slender Man or such into an article.

item.title: This has kind of already been done
item.content:encoded: Lovecraft, Cthulhu in the SCP Foundation universe, there's already an entry like this in the Project Crossover hub: SCP-1926-R

item.title: Re: It’s cool, but
item.content:encoded:
I don’t think it’s a positive thing for this site

I'm pretty sure you're in the minority there.

item.title: Re: It’s cool, but
item.content:encoded:
Skips with narratives are typically more interesting to the modern userbase than ones without.

This isn't even a new thing by any means, this has been the norm for a good long while.

item.title: It’s cool, but
item.content:encoded: It’s cool and I like it, but it feels like a story book, not an SCP. I’ve noticed that a lot with recent SCP’s and I have to say I don’t think it’s a positive thing for this site. Stories are fine, but stick to story sites. It varies a little too much from SCP I believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not a bad article, the writing is better than ok, but….
The diary parts, particulary those of Howard don't feel like they'd be written by Lovecraft.
Even for his personal diary and not for a story to be sold, it doesn't feel like Lovecraft. It's too literal and too modern. He could weave emotion and dread in a sentence over and over again without it getting old. These do not do that. I realise, again, it's supposedly his diary and not one of his stories but I doubt he'd respond the way he does portrayed in these segments if he'd actually dream of Old Gods. From everything i've gathered, they don't care about humanity or picking on humans (with the exception of a rare few in universe characters (Al Hazred for example)).
I can enjoy crossovers but I don't believe this credits Lovecraft as much as it could/should.
Personally, I downvote it on these remarks.
Skip wise (except the writing): content, formatting, keeping the reader gripped on reading, that's all fine.

Just not enough "Lovecraft" for me.
A reluctant downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title on the mainlist has been edited to its proper title. Thank you for pointing this out, Reverend :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was aware that a decent amount of people would see the article like this, and I completely understand why.

I would like to say, however, the article’s not intended to be entirely accurate to Lovecraft’s works in the real world. I felt like just having them be exactly like Lovecraft’s actual portrayal was just making spooky scary murder monsters instead of things that deliberately drive people crazy and are attempting to enter our world through people’s dreams. Doing this just gave me more to work with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lovecraft described his creatures as indifferent. They view us the way we view ants. Their existence is a threat to humanity not because they are malevolent, but because they are so incomprehensible that simply looking at them can drive a person insane. In this article, not only are they malevolent, they're actively toying with Lovecraft and the people around him for entertainment.

Downvote. This article is well-written, but I can't get over the portrayal of Lovecraft's creatures as cruel gods who amuse themselves by interfering with the lives of humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just wanted to point out that this is still [ACCESS DENIED] on the mainlist.

+1 though. Not a bad skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have wanted to like this. But I am not a fan of crossovers, particularly those that actually try to make one fictional universe actually part of another. In this case, I like Lovecraft and SCP as completely separate entities. To allow Lovecraftian monsters to be real and existing in the SCP universe even if, as noted above, it's simply from Lovecraft himself being a conduit for them to exist, throws me off badly. I tried to like it, but I couldn't. It'd be fine as a tale, but not as a mainline SCP entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor nitpick, Hastur, Chaugnar Faugn, and Tsathoggua were made by Robert W. Chambers, Frank Belknap Long, and Clark Ashton Smith, respectively, not Lovecraft. Maybe you know that, but since this is about Lovecraft I'd have stuck to his creations. Is the unlucky shapeshifting starfish supposed to be Nyarlathotep?

Also, while I love Lovecraft's stuff, I don't feel like this was particularly inspired or provided any real twist. It's pretty much just "Old Ones are real, using Lovecraft as a conduit," which I've seen several times before. It just so happens that the SCP Foundation is there this time, but it didn't really add anything new to the concept. If I'd written this, I'd have made it so the Old Ones didn't actually exist until Lovecraft wrote them, then became more and more powerful as more people read his works, so the Foundation contains them by destroying Lovecraftian fiction that isn't very original either. I'm sure there's some way to do S.C.P. Lovecraft justice, and I don't know what that is, but I know what it isn't.

A very reluctant downvote. I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy mother.

The fact that you turned all of Lovecraftian mythology into an SCP is impressive. And while the writing itself could use a little more work, this is a pretty stellar1 article.

Also, I'm really glad that someone here knows about The King in Yellow:

The entity was humanoid and robed in a cloak of yellow hue. The entity's face and its body could not be viewed due to said parts being obscured by the cloak, but seventeen long "tentacle-like" appendages used for locomotion sprouted from the bottom of the cloak, seemingly acting as legs

Like seagull said above: This is a great article that has that Series I-era feel with a modern twist to the whole thing. Like a finely aged cheese.

Oh and the fact that you managed to get Gears to collaborate with you is an impressive feat too.

Good job you two.

+1 with the force of Azathoth's herald's demonic, monotonous piping.

Footnotes
1. Pun intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't know what I expected with Gears' first skip in years, but whatever I did expect, this blew me away. The descriptions and the story have a Series I feel, yet manage to bring modern quality. I've only read a marginal amount of Lovecraft, but I still loved this one.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to upvote this so badly simply because it's skippified Lovecraft, but it's just… skippified Lovecraft, which makes it more literal and mundane than anything Lovecraft (or SCP, ideally) should be. It's well-written, don't get me wrong, and a loving tribute, but I was just hoping for a twist or something "more" that never happened. Honestly, there's nothing here that I couldn't get from reading Lovecraft's stories, which isn't good for an article directed at those familiar with them.

Side note, the list of instances with their "capabilities" kinda feels (to me, anyway) like something out of a roleplaying game rather than an SCP document. Maybe just replacing the word "capabilities" with "notes" may help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought I'd never see this scp again. I'm happy you posted it again, moreso with the changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some oddly structured sentences to my eye, but I guess that's appropriate given the subject matter. Very well done.

item.title: Author Post - S.C.P. Lovecraft
item.content:encoded:
I'm starting to get the feeling that I'm reading Lovecraftian nonfiction.

After a bit of brainstorming, I thought of H.P. Lovecraft for the 4k contest (yes, we started that long ago). The original concept for this article was very similar to the one we have now, but I just had the skip be Providence rather than an event that takes us to an extradimensional Providence. I wanted to go for something creepy yet historical while using the concept of H.P. Lovecraft essentially being nonfiction, so who did I go to?

The legend himself, Dr Gears.

This is actually a collaboration between Gears and I. Without him, this article wouldn't have had as much fine tuning and creep factor as it did. It's been an absolutely fantastic journey working with Gears and I wouldn't have wanted this to turn out any different than what it is.

Many thanks to JackalRelated, Prototype_Toaster, Dr Aers, Mortos, and stormbreath for critique and some more finetuning.

Image sources are here and here with both being licensed under Creative Commons.

You can find more of my work here and more of Gears' work here.

Thanks for reading!
